questions.text: My nervous system seems to have a wide comfort zone that few experiences push me out of.
I believe that decisiveness is one of my core strengths.
I feel that my personal workspace should be optimized for my needs, not shared convenience.
I notice the way someone's posture changes depending on whether they are speaking to a peer or someone they perceive as above or below them.
I tend to choose the harder path if it clears the way for someone else.
I plan my route before leaving for an unfamiliar destination.
I feel that my emotional intensity sometimes makes it hard to think clearly.
I routinely research and pursue opportunities that specifically strengthen my resume or portfolio.
I like to take time and let the group deliberate before a decision is made.
I believe that maintaining my analytical perspective during someone's emotional crisis is one of the most constructive things I can offer.
I can watch an intensely emotional film and feel moved without being shaken.
I find that the moral concern I have for people in pain is a compass that reliably points me in the right direction.
I do not naturally notice who is comfortable and who is uncomfortable in a group.
My emotional responses have a muted quality compared to what I observe in others.
I believe that the willingness to take suffering into account — to genuinely let it weigh — is what distinguishes moral seriousness from moral posturing.
I feel that my moral compass is most engaged when I am confronted with another person's undeserved hardship.
I notice when a person tilts their head while listening because they are genuinely trying to understand versus when they tilt it to appear attentive.
I detect when a person's friendliness toward me is calibrated to what they think I can offer them.
I can feel adrenaline surge through me in response to relatively minor provocations.
I find that writing things down helps me commit to them and hold myself accountable.
I would rather risk overstepping than under-contributing in a crisis.
I rarely redefine objectives that were set by the group.
I send messages quickly and correct mistakes afterward if they matter.
I take detailed meeting notes and circulate them afterward.
I tend to miss when a person's enthusiasm about a topic is meant to signal something about their values.
I am drawn to being the person who helps the group make tough calls collectively.
I hold the view that comfort shared is comfort doubled, and comfort hoarded is comfort halved.
I find it hard to tell when someone is impressed versus when they are just being encouraging.
I believe that the capacity to process human tragedy without being emotionally destabilized is essential for anyone who wants to help at scale.
I hold the view that the measure of my character is how I treat people who can offer me nothing.
I find that I am at my sharpest when I can engage with a human problem without my emotions influencing my assessment.
I believe that a well-developed sense of personal ambition is attractive and admirable.
I find that not knowing what comes next is exciting rather than stressful.
I naturally gravitate toward being the person who introduces people and assigns them roles.
I tend to feel the full weight of an experience rather than experiencing it at a distance.
I believe that not everything worth doing can or should be measured.
